speaker: interviewer
text: It's February the 5th, 1996. The survivor is Rosette Fischer Cygelfarb-- Mary Rothschild, interviewer. It's in Los Angeles, California, USA. It will be in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's February 5, 1996. I'm Mary Rothschild interviewing Rosette Fischer for the survivors of the Shoah Visual History Foundation. It's February 5th, 1996, tape 1 with Rosette Fischer. Please give us your name and spell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Rosette Fischer-- R-O-S-E-T-T-E. Fischer is F-I-S-C-H-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cygelfarb. Want me to spell it?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes please.

speaker: interviewee
text: C-Y-G-E-L-F-A-R-B.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your-- did you have any nicknames when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birthday? September the 23rd, 1923.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lodz, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember a very, very happy home. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SOBBING] My father was very kind. We were three girls. And he would always make surprises, like come home with some presents for my mother, for us. He would tell my mother, close your eyes. And we bring her towards the mirror. And then he said, now you can look. There would be a little fur coat, a necklace, toys for us. And he never, never screamed at us. When he talked to us, he-- his voice was very even. [SOBBING] And, and we respected him and loved him so much.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Scmul. My son is named after my, my father and the father of my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did he make a, a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father-- we had a store from clothing, women's clothing. And in 1936, business were very bad. He went to Paris. There was the exhibition at the time. And they decide that between my mother, the family, to move to Paris. In 1937, towards the end or beginning of '38. I think it was still '37-- we moved to Paris. My parents went first. And then they came to get my little sister and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ester.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about your two sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sisters-- mine elder sister's name is Cecile. In Poland, it was Cecilya. And my younger sister, Rachele-- Rachele Lea. And my granddaughter is named after her, [SOBBING] which I'm very grateful to my son and my daughter-in-law.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you moved to Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 15 or--

speaker: interviewer
text: How was life in Paris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Life in Paris-- at first I didn't know the language. But everybody-- I got friends right away. I guess that it's easy for me to make friends. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was busy with my friends. I went to school [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for a year. Then the war, of course, broke out. We were in hiding. Then we got back to Paris. Where did you go to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hiding? Outskirt of-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] oh, I forgot the name. I'm too excited. It's outskirt of Paris.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we went into hiding?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we heard that the Germans are coming. So we went into hiding in exactly-- no. At first, we were supposed to go to the zone libre, which is the free zone. But at the same day, the, the car broke down. And we couldn't get it repaired. So my, my, my father said, this is [NON-ENGLISH], that we should not go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we stayed. And at the beginning, the Germans just didn't do that much till they started to, you know, to take the papers. They took away the papers from my father who had the papers, the commercant.

speaker: interviewee
text: That means that he could only make commerce. And they wouldn't give him any other papers, like working papers. I got working papers. And when the war broke out and, and-- I mean, when the German came, and my father couldn't do much, so I inquired for a job, which will permit my-- us to stay because without a job, there wouldn't be anything. Right after-- I didn't even have the permit, they arrested us, two--

speaker: interviewer
text: Who arrested you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two French civilian detectives, I guess. I was ready to go out from the house. A friend of mine would come and pick me up. And I opened-- I thought it was him. And I knocked at the-- and somebody knocked at the door. And there were two Germans that didn't let us go out. And my father tried everything. They should just go. But it didn't work. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We, we walked, all the four of us-- my little sister, my mother, and my father, and me-- to the prefecture. And I have to say that they probably didn't-- didn't want us as much as they wanted my parents. They took us to a depot, you know, a la mairie. And there, they even sent us out for some bread. But we wouldn't leave our parents. Their French was poor. And we wouldn't leave, no matter what.

speaker: interviewee
text: So from there, we went a la prefecture. And from the prefecture, they sent us to Drancy. Drancy was a camp outskirt of Paris.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the only good thing about Drancy was that the family was together. We did not sleep together. But we had my father eating with us, and so on. Our friends still could send a package from here and there. And at the time that we were there, there weren't too many deportations. But one day-- one day, came a convoy with children. [CRYING] The children were-- there was a place where [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] usually, I guess, that was for the soldiers that was to make gym or whatever, sports. And that was [SIGHS] in the middle of the camp. We were not allowed to see. But I had the time to see a little boy holding his sister. [SOBBING] The little sister would hold her doll against-- close her doll. And the children would walk. We didn't know what it was, why they're walking to-- from the paper-- they would have names on it. Later on, we find out what happened to them. But that always stays in my mind. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The 9th of June, 1923-- oh, wait, no. June-- I think, yes, we're in 1923. We all were deported to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we talk about that, can you describe the conditions of Drancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Drancy, the conditions were-- there were barracks, like-- everybody slept on wooden beds. And to wash, there was a whole row all along, with the faucet-- how you call? Faucets. And we would wash together because late-- the women will be with women. And men will be separate. We got very, you know, not that much to eat. But in comparison with, with, with Auschwitz and Birkenau, I think it was a paradise.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't remember. It didn't matter too much. We were with my father. And, like I say, our friend would send us something from time to time. I really don't remember what we ate. There was a lady that was with us who sang beautifully. And when we were deported, the same lady, when-- she starts screaming into the wagons-- we were deported in wagons from-- [FRENCH]-- cattle wagons. And she, she went crazy, like. You know, she started screaming, they're going to kill us! They're going to kill us! Somebody slapped her over the face. And she sat down. And she was just talking to herself. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just unbelievable. The atmosphere-- one sat on top of the others-- and the little windows. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we, we were squeezed. But we still thought that we're going to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I must have been 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Drancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost 20. Hmm?

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in Drancy?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Drancy, we stayed six months.

speaker: interviewer
text: Six months. What was a typical day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was--

speaker: interviewer
text: In Drancy. What did you do during the day?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] We, we had-- people made shows. As a matter of fact, I was in the-- one little shows-- was nothing to it. And we tried to read. We made friends, which I still have the one that survived. We're still very, very close. And we'd see the family, try to have news from the outside. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I had some friends-- my-- Doris, which was my best girlfriend. Her parents were with her also. And we would be together and, and just read-- and talking about what we read and what we did. And there was a very nice man that took-- that had a non-Jewish wife. And he was also share with us and give us some things that he got from his wife from Paris. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It, it was every day [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just like a detention camp. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Drancy was somewhere where I hope I'm going to get out from. Because my friends from outside also were sending me letters and were saying that they do what they can to liberate us. It didn't happen. But they tried. So I guess-- so we had more hope in Dancy-- Drancy, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you on the cattle trains?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe we were three nights, three nights. That was a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my younger sister, my mother, and I, and, of course, my friends that we made. I have here a friend that-- his name is Morris Goldberg. And he was with his family, too. And they retarded the deportation for one day because he spilled some coffee on his leg. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] but they were yelling. And, you know-- you know, I guess that I don't have to talk about that because everybody knows what was going on. There was no place where to go to the toilet, if you'll excuse me. And we were squeezed. And people were going mad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was just unreal, just unreal. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. They told us we're going to, to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we arrived in Auschwitz, there was, of course, screaming, yelling, a lot of SS in uniforms. They start to separate people-- back-- to the right, to the left. They separated my father from us. My mother-- we took, my sister and I, took my mother in the middle. They were looking at us. And they said-- they looked at my mother, looked at her. And they said, well, I think you still can work. We didn't know what it means.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother must have been 44. She was a young woman, very pretty. As a matter of fact, when I think about it, they used to say, don't work with your mother because [LAUGHS] it doesn't do you justice. She was very pretty and very good. My mother and I were constantly in camp-- I mean, together in the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened that first day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not talking today. The first day? The first day, we were pushed together into the camp and then into unlousing place. We didn't know what it was. Everybody-- they took away all, all our belongings, all our little-- whatever we had. They cut our hair. They didn't cut. They shaved our heads. They said that we're going to have a shower. But we-- I don't even remember if we had a shower. Then we stood in the line alphabetically. And they made a tattoo on our arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got tattoo. My sister got tattooed, my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can we see it? Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [CRYING] I guess. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was-- [SOBBING] I didn't feel human anymore. It was very, very-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very bad. That was, I think, the worst thing what they did to us. We did not feel like human beings anymore. [SOBBING] They took away the pride, took away everything that a person, ever a person could have. [CRYING] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you please ask some questions because it's hard for me to-- What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened next? Yeah, we went to a big barrack. There were little girls from Slovakia, I guess.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother, my sister and I and some of our friends. And they give us places that were under-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH]. And it was all wooden. And we were supposed to sleep there. When we sat down, a young girl came and said something nasty to my mother. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what-- what language?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think was Slovakian, or-- I really don't remember. Because she slapped my mother's face. Because my mother said, you're so young. Why are you-- why are you talking like that? And she didn't like it. So she slapped my mother, of course. I went to my mother's rescue, and I got beating. So that taught me already, first day, what's going on that-- even young people have no respect for older people. And, there, of course, people that were there probably longer, whatever, told me, don't you dare do something like this. Just keep it to yourself. Then we would get a ration of bread. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother cut it in three. And she didn't let us eat everything at once. Of course, we were younger. We wanted to. So she would give us a portion from hers. [SOBBING] When we saw this, we said, no way. So we listened to her. And we would eat our portion at, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the time when she wanted. There were-- we were-- at first, we were taken to work, to-- outside. We would take-- we would take stones from one place and put it another one. Then we would-- the following day, we would do-- take them and put them back. There was-- just to make us tired and nothing constructive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: One day-- we were wearing pants, all pants, with a khaki top, and-- which has a cross on the back from paint-- a cross in the back. And then we had kerchiefs, some kind of kerchief that everybody weared because we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of shoes were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, we had wooden shoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What time of the year was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was June.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1943. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] One day-- I always had a full face and looked healthy. So I would always be the first one when they counted us to go to work. One day, we were not sent to work. And they would make selections. We didn't know for what-- we didn't know for what. But then we learned that this was for some people that didn't-- I don't-- didn't like, or whatever, they would send to gas chambers. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My younger sister got sick one day. It was very hot. It was August already. I don't know if it was meningitis or what. She got sick, and she didn't talk anymore. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We took her to the barracks. And then, from there, they took her to a-- another camp, which was adjacent to our camp and to, like, a hospital. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. She was very pretty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SOBBING] I had a friend that I found when we marched to work that would-- this was three months later, I think-- that would send us little packages through somebody. And he said that he will send something. His name was Simon Goodman. He recognized me from France. And he threw-- over the wires, he threw some shoes. Each day, he threw something else. And when my sister went to the hospital, she survived only four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead. What happened to your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister went to that [NON-ENGLISH], this hospital. And I wanted very badly to see her. One day, I got somebody to take me there. And I was free from work because they're kind of-- some of the people respected my mother and me, maybe because they didn't have a mother. [SOBBING] So they kind of regarded us, you know, better. I mean, they, they liked us. So they let me go to the other camp to see my sister. When I arrived, the-- to the [NON-ENGLISH], they told me, well, your sister just died. [SOBBING] I, I couldn't forgive myself for not being there before. And I ask, I want to see her, please. And they said you cannot see her. She's better off. You know, they took-- the, the nurse took me around. She said, believe me, she's better off. You should be happy that she's gone. I said, but I want to see her. So they took me to a kind of a-- it was like a wooden shack or something. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they said, well, she's there.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I would like you to open the door. They said they don't have the key. So I looked-- I said, can I look through? And I looked. And I see-- I thought I seen my sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: I see so many [SOBBING] women, so many people, one on top of the other, with their eyes open, [SOBBING] with their arms spread and leg one on-- I thought I saw my sister. I still think I did see my-- it was horrible. I could not forgive myself for not have seen her before. When I went back and my mother came from work, because she had gone to work, I put on a face. And I told my mother that my sister went to another camp, that they took care of her, that my friend made it all possible. And she went to another camp. So I don't know if my mother believed me or not. But she didn't say anything. [CRYING] [SOBBING] Another time, when we worked together with my mother, somebody that was already-- we worked indoors. We worked in a Weberei, where we were making supplies for soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was-- we made long strips of some kind of rubber. They said it was for tanks. And we had to deliver that much every day. But with the time, some of our friends find a way to mark it-- you know, and to take off the mark, so we wouldn't be punished that we didn't make that much that day. [SIGHS] My mother had made another kind of work in the same barrack. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] One day, a man came in from the men's-- because they would, would go-- bringing in materials for us to work. That man always used to call me, bring a little something for us from that friend that I found. It would be a little bit of sugar, sometimes a little bit of margarine or whatever. And I would share with my friend next to me and my mother, of course.

speaker: interviewee
text: One day he came very happy, the same man. And he said, you know what? Your father is alive. Your father came from Jaworzno to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you shouldn't worry. He was sick. He has diarrhea. But you should not worry because we're going to take care of him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the man left, I don't know why, I felt a pang in my heart. I was very happy at the time. But then I got very sad. I don't know why. I just couldn't believe my happiness.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the next day, I didn't hear from the man at all. So I turned to my-- to my girlfriend, which worked next to me, and I said, [SOBBING] how come you didn't tell me that my father [SOBBING] died? [SOBBING] And she said to me, I didn't want to tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had that feeling that he must have died the same moment when I felt that pang in my heart. Because the next day, I just felt that something-- too much happiness for me. It-- I, I just can't believe it, it did happen. But I want to believe that my father knew, through my friend, that we are alive. Again, I did not tell my mother. I told my mother that he is in camp and taken care of. My mother, I don't know. She was always telling me, we have to survive. [SOBBING] We have to survive. We have to tell the world what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to my mother, no. What do you have to survive for? Let's go on the wires. Let's finish because [SOBBING] people don't care. They just don't care for us. They don't care what happened. Because if they did, they will-- would be human. That wouldn't happen what happened, what's going on. Why we have to go on like this?

speaker: interviewee
text: But she-- I-- like I say, I don't know if she knew that my father was dead or not, I don't know. But she had that will, that strong will. She said no, no. In Yiddish she used to talk to me. No, my [NON-ENGLISH], please, [NON-ENGLISH]-- she would call me [NON-ENGLISH]. Please, you're going to see. We're going to survive. We're going to survive. We have to tell people what-- [SOBBING] what's going on and what happened. I couldn't leave it alone. So I wouldn't go. And so I stayed for her. [SOBBING] And that's how we stayed in camp. And very often when we used to go to work, a young fellow would-- a SS. And that was before. I forgot. That was before. They would-- they would have dogs with them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they would just make fun. Like, there was a river. They would throw in straw into the river and would tell the girls that did not know how to swim, go and get it. And they will just laugh. And they would take the gun and shoot in the air just to scare us. Because the girls wouldn't want to go. We wouldn't want to go into that water. So he would take the gun and he'd-- and they were laughing and laughing. They were having so much fun.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just don't know how human beings can do that. That's what I was saying to my mother. What for, and what for? [SOBBING] German or not German, it didn't matter. A, a person is a person. [SOBBING] I'd say, how-- it's, it's just unbelievable.
